text: The surgeon uses a ligature device to dissect the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus. A 37mm ligature atlas is employed, and the supra-umbilical port is utilized instead of the umbilical port.

The surgeon dissects the fallopian tube and major salpings, followed by the ovarian ligament on the right adnexal structure. The large uterus requires the laparoscopic telescope to maneuver over it to access deeper recesses, with port positions arranged according to the baseball diamond concept.
The surgeon prepares to separate the round ligament on the left side, adjusting the port placement due to the large uterus, which requires deviation from conventional laparoscopic port positions.

The surgeon uses a ligature atlas device to manipulate the fallopian tube, which is currently being preserved. The fallopian tube is visible hanging near the uterus, potentially obstructing the view of the uterine artery and the posterior cul-de-sac.

The surgeon positions the light cable of the 30-degree telescope at approximately the eleven o'clock position to enhance visibility while dissecting near the ovarian ligament. The camera angle provides a view over the bowel to minimize bleeding risk by staying close to the ovarian ligament rather than moving towards the uterus.

The surgeon pushes the uterus towards the five o'clock position while using a harmonic scalpel to separate the uterovesical fold. The left hand stretches the peritoneum anteriorly, revealing a clear areolar plane for dissection.

The surgeon demonstrates a clear plane of dissection at the UV fold, with the retroverted uterus being manipulated using a uterine manipulator. The anatomical orientation is adjusted to facilitate the dissection process.

The surgeon uses the Rumi uterine manipulator, a full circle colpotomizer, to gain control over the uterus. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is being separated, which moves the ureter laterally. The vaginal fascia and peritoneum are also separated. If minor bleeding occurs, it is controlled using bipolar or harmonic devices. The uterus is manipulated from the five o'clock to seven o'clock position.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device to open the anterior leaf of the broad ligament on the right side, with the left hand stretching the peritoneum upward and the right hand hooking the tissue anterolaterally. The harmonic device vibrates at high frequency, facilitating a precise dissection plane without bleeding, allowing for the separation of the bladder and complete opening of the anterior leaf.

The surgeon identifies the advantageous fissure tissue around the uterine artery and cervicovaginal fissure. The remaining upper pedicles of the broad ligament are separated with careful traction applied during the dissection.

The surgeon utilizes two ipsilateral and one contralateral port to approach the uterine artery. Some pedicles of the broad ligament remain and need to be separated. The anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament may be opened to enhance coagulation using the ligature.

The surgeon uses the vibrating jaw of the harmonic device to hook and cut the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The uterine artery is coagulated using a ligature, with multiple applications to prevent bleeding. The vein, located posteriorly to the artery, is also coagulated carefully.
The surgeon exposes the lumen of the uterine structure, with the vein visible posteriorly. The mechanoid ligament is then addressed in the dissection process.

The surgeon adjusts the ligature on the right side to face towards the cervix, ensuring a medial approach to the uterine artery. Ascending branches are carefully coagulated to prevent reverse bleeding, with particular attention to avoid ureter injury.
The surgeon performs a lateral colpotomy using a harmonic scalpel, cutting through the vaginal fascia and some of the mechanoid tissue.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to perform a lateral colpotomy, smoothly cutting through the vaginal tissue and progressing towards the opposite side. The tip of the harmonic is positioned above the uterosacral ligament, avoiding cutting the vaginal part of the ligament.

The surgeon performs a posterior colpotomy, revealing the uterosacral ligament attached to the vaginal part. The ligament is left intact to prevent wall prolapse, while the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament is carefully cut at the level just touching the cervix.

The surgeon completes the dissection on the right side, transitioning to the left side of the vaginal vault and cervical part of the uterosacral ligament. The entire uterus is maneuvered into the uterine manipulator, indicating the completion of this surgical step.

The surgeon performs the hysterectomy, bisecting the large uterus with two speculums. The uterus is slowly extracted, with some remaining attached tissue being separated using a harmonic device. The assistant manipulates the uterus to aid in its removal.

The surgeon carefully bisects the large uterus for removal, using a Dandy jamming knot with continuous locking sutures to secure the uterosacral corners. A sponge is visible inside the gloves, which are used to pack the vagina and prevent gas leakage.

The surgeon uses a glove to prevent gas leakage during the procedure. After addressing the posterior aspect, attention is turned to the anterior leaf, ensuring inclusion of the vaginal epithelium to prevent granulation tissue formation. The needle is passed through the loop in a dandy jamming knot, which automatically locks.

The surgeon demonstrates a starter knot technique, which securely locks without easily unlocking. The assistant holds the knot while the surgeon performs continuous suturing, sequentially taking the posterior and anterior leaf of the vaginal vault.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the vaginal vault, using interlocking techniques to ensure secure closure. Various suturing methods are mentioned, including extracorporeal square knot, barb suture, and tumble square knot, with the use of a vial PDS for suturing. The suturing progresses with the second and third bites being taken.

In the total laparoscopic hysterectomy, the surgeon positions the ports supra-umbilically and in the right and left iliac fossae, ensuring a 7.5 cm distance between them. The ports form an arc, allowing instruments to be at least 18 cm away from the uterine artery, which is the target of dissection.

During the closure of the vaginal vault, the surgeon uses a type three lever technique, which may cause slight tremor due to the magnified movement and rectified force. The corners are approximated, and the tail of the dundee jamming knot is kept large to facilitate easy approximation.

The surgeon approximates the corners of the vaginal vault using a surgeon's knot, with a two-one-one double wrap followed by two alternating wraps. The corners are brought closer together, providing support to the vault and approximating the uterosacral ligaments. Scissors are used to cut the suture, and suction is applied, with minimal blood loss observed.
